sentences.text: Yes.
My sisters, the older one married in Krasnik to a gentleman with the name Kestenbaum, Avram Kestenbaum.
And the younger one married in Krasnik, to a ex-Russian soldier, what dropped the uniform over there, changed.
Arthur Feder. ?] [?
Arthur Feder ?] is alive.
Kestenbaum, unfortunately, is not.
Kestenbaum is K-E-S-T-E-N-B-A-U-M.
And Feder is F-E-D-E-R.
They both went to Krakow, opened up a restaurant.
Right away, after the war.
And from there, they went with-- through almost the same route, through Austria-- Czechoslovakia, Austria, and to France.
The older sister  was living in Paris.
The younger one went with her husband straight to Israel.
In 1947, she was already in Israel.
When I arrived, she was already in America.
To America.
So we were like this, separated all the time.
No.
When I went to Israel, she went from Paris to the United State.
Yes.
Not-- no, I didn't.
That if you didn't hear from somebody 30 years, obvious, you have the conclusion what could happen.
First of all, it was still in the underground in Poland.
There were no communications to each unit.
You cannot know what happened.
There were many fights with the Germans as they were retreating from the Russian front.
And we didn't know if he survived or not.
And you didn't hear, 30 years, you assume he's not alive.
I started on the farm.
But you could not live from the farm if you have no anything.
Just have field and a small, little house, like a chicken coop at a house, with a bathroom outside.
I built the first shower that-- from a tank, like a water tank.
And under  it, I made a kerosene lamp.
And we were looking to-- going out for jobs.
After two, three weeks [? cutting ?] [? oranges ?] by the older farmers, through my good connection, I got a job at the Machon Weizmann, when President Weizmann was alive.
At the garden, in those-- by the flowers, in the beginning.
And then, in the Institute for Research, also Machon Weizmann.
Each box was sprayed for different chemicals, to find out which is the-- the longer preserves from them, from getting spoiled.
Maybe I worked about two months.
And I thanked for the job, and I left it.
And everybody said, such a easy job.
I said, no, I will be a farmer.
I will go work on the farm.
I had the farm.
I said, I will not keep fields like this.
So I started growing tomatoes, potatoes.
When I-- when the potatoes were ready, I left them to be rotten in the field.
I didn't pay to take them out.
It was too much potatoes.
But I started also, with cows, milk cows and calves to grow for meat, like steers.
And after this was-- we made a living, but a very hard living.
It was from day to day.
The worst thing was that every month didn't pass by, I shouldn't be draft to the army.
And I belonged to the commander, the way I mentioned.
One of them had the commander jeep to pick up his nine soldiers and come in a half an hour to be in the uniform, from the time they pick you up from home.
Yeah, but the-- but the big one like this.
You used muscle.
I was doing three, fours a day.
So you have to have muscles to do it.
Put it on the wagon with the horse, bring it inside.
So after this, I started my-- growing chickens.
I wind up having 6,000, 8,000 at a time, which was in Israel, selling 1,000 every three to four weeks from me to Tel Aviv, and getting in 1,000 youngsters.
And that all was modern made, like stairs, three stairs, where the [? vase ?] was going down.
The food and the water was going automatically.
Then I discovered-- and we had, already, electricity.
I pulled in speakers all over where the chickens were, and gave them music in the night.
So they couldn't sleep.
They were eating more.
So they grew faster.
Everybody was laughing.
But then they were copying me.
[LAUGHS] Summertime, it's very hot.
So I pulled a sprinkler system on the roofs, on the metal roofs, where the chickens were.
Keep daytime, watering.
So it was cool.
It kept them OK.
And I was successful until 1956, when I was called to the army, the Egyptian-Israeli War of 1956.
At that time, all the men went to the army.
The men went to the army.
Women don't buy chickens.
They don't cook.
They were worried what happened there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --off.
And I packed several trucks of chickens to Tel Aviv, sold it out.
I came back 12 hours later when mine-- what I should.
But they all know me, that I was OK, in the army.
I went in.
I showed them the invoices, what I did.
They didn't do nothing.
But when the actual war started, I went out with-- for patrol.
They sent us, as a patrol, six tanks and three half tracks.
All together, 100 guy.
They called them pluga in the Israeli Army.
And behind us was moving-- the whole thing, this was around four o'clock in the morning.
At one place, we came over, the name of the place is Abu Ageila.
It's in Arabic, that comes out, the name, Abu Ageila.
We came very close.
And in one moment, everything was burning.
They let us very close.
They had a fortress built 8 kilometers long and 40 width, built by the Germans.
With the good, and the best knowledge of the Israeli intelligence, they did not know about this fortress.
Otherwise we wouldn't go  in straight on it.
When they opened the fires, or-- oh yeah, on the way there, in the middle of the road-- I have to back-- go back a little bit.
We were stopped.
And I was switched from the tank to a half track.
The reason was that the guy, what was driving the half track, became sick.
And I knew both vehicles to maneuver.
And they had guys who they knew to drive the tank.
So I was switched to the half track.
The headquarters didn't know from the switch.
This was down on the command on the spot, on the road.
The tanks burned without recognition.
The only half track came back almost whole end without damage was mine, what I was driving.
Not, for this, I was a good driver, having a wonderful officer.
And he instructed to throw smoke grenades.
And in Israeli Army, you don't leave any injured people on the field.
Not, like I understand, when the rescue was in-- in the-- in-- in Iran, when Carter was President, what kind of mess they made over there.
This does not exist.
And we came back, 34 without legs, without hand, not-- I was not touched. 34 from 100.
It didn't last a half an hour, and Ben Gurion and Dayan came in, flew in, in the first line.
They were sitting and asking what happened, to find out.
And I don't know what all this, it was, what they give after this.
The planes start bombing for three days with-- [? at a roar ?] on their fortress.
And the end of the third day, we set up-- yeah, what they call it-- lines.
Every 50 feet, another line of soldiers.
But they picked, in the first line, the best units.
We found out that the Arabs in the fortress have no water anymore.
And they were-- and one day, they run out with the bayonets on us too.
And they came out in the thousands from there.
And we were working very good with the machine guns.
And finally, we took to them alive, more than 1,100, after the-- after the several hours fighting.
When we inspected their bunkers, to our dismay, none of them were damaged from all the bombing.
None of them.
You could drive a jeep in there.
They-- they made one mistake.
The water when they pulled, is from a place where Moses hit the stone and got water.
They call it in Arabic and Hebrew, [?
Al-Qassim. ?] It's 50 kilometers away from the fortress.
Our parachute jumped over there and cut off the water.
And this made them give up or final fight-- they would try to go out of this.
[LAUGHS] And at that time, I didn't know when we reached, already, the [? sewer ?] canal after this.
This was-- you know, it took us 17 hours to reach the [? sewer ?] canal.
When we drove the trucks, the half tracks, we got gasoline with the ropes from the helicopters giving us to the half tracks to pull in and continue going, not to stop.
You know, everybody was wondering.
Maybe something happened, home.
They gave me a helicopter ride to Beersheba.
From Beersheba, I was catching trams.
Like, on the military, they take you on the road.
Not like now, where they're afraid to take.
When I arrived in town, it's a old town, [?
Talmei Bilu. ?] And it's the new one, where I was living.
So when I was walking through the old town, whoever that saw me, they hugged me.
What happened, the mailman was a good friend of mine.
He had a telegram.
He walked to to the door.
He couldn't deliver it.
He walked back.
So she didn't know nothing, what happened.
[LAUGHS] And then, after 24 hours, I went back again.
We came to the [? sewer ?] canal.
We were ready to pass the [? sewer ?] canal.
We had the trucks and the landing boats ready to-- to drown the trucks.
And the boat will go over with the tank, on the other side.
John Foster Dulles was the American Secretary of State.
We had strict orders to go back.
And we know it was all the Russian based.
They found oil in the barrels, buried in the sand, prepared for war.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So I survived, that time, the Israeli  Army.
Tell me-- you mentioned that you and your wife had three children.
When?
And what were their names?
Faye, Sylvia, and Dina.
1951, Faye. 1954, Sylvia.
And 1956, two weeks before I went to the war, Dina.
At that time, we had no money to buy food for the-- to start to grow a chicken again, when they mostly died.
There was no insurance.
Now they do have insurance in Israel.
At that time there was no insurance.
I understand you have to go to the army.
But you have to cover somebody what has such a losses.
And you have to start building, again, the same thing.
But I went to a supplier, to Rehovot.
His name was [INAUDIBLE].
I got back from the army.
I said, [?
Zwe, ?] could you help me?
He said, what do you need?
I said, I want to start again.
He said, you really want to start?
The price is so low.
I said, you don't start when the price is high.
I will start when the price is low.
But I don't have money.
And if I don't succeed with the chicken, I will not have money to pay you until a year, I still have 10 calves, until they grow up.
And they will be one-year-old.
I think they will cover the foot.
He said, anything you want, you have it.
When the first chicken were ready, it took three months.
The price tripled.
And I started making plenty money at that time.
This was 1956, '57.
And I decide to build a big house for ourself.
Leave the house as it is and start building.
So I had grape trees all around the house.
So one place, I decided to cut out the trees.
And as I was standing and cutting the trees, came the mailman with a letter from the US Embassy.
If I don't pick up the visa, I will lose my term.
I got registered about five years earlier.
[LAUGHS] And I said to myself, maybe somebody calls me again.
[LAUGHS]
So I decided to come to the State.
I came by myself.
Six months later came my wife, with the children.
A year later, we had another boy here, in America, in 1960.
His name is Larry.
He has two grandson, two boys, which two grandson.
He was born in Brooklyn, New York.
He is--
Yes.
He's the only a Yankee.
And over there, the  first six months, I couldn't get a job.
I wrote a letter to my wife that I left a broom in Israel.
But there's plenty of dollars laying on the street.
But if you are without a broom, you have to bend down.
You hurt your back.
[LAUGHS]
She was laughing.
And she said, do you really need that?
You see?
We had so good here.
Why?
And she was right.
There-- and-- and the-- I was-- I could consider from the all hundred farmers, we were the richest over there.
We had no loans.
The all previous loans were paid off.
And I was the youngest.
I knew right away, when I went out on the farm, if the chickens, when they were sleeping, I know if-- what kind of sickness they have.
I learned from the-- from doctors, when they came in.
Many people were asking me.
I was teaching them.
And-- at-- but you decide to go.
We went.
Then I start being, here, a butcher, in Brooklyn, working in the freezers from four o'clock in the morning.
One guy told me, Nathan, never learn too good.
I said, why?
They will never fire you if you're no good to learn.
They'll give you an increase every time.
And you will be, always, a butcher.
So don't learn good.
I didn't listen to him.
I did learn good.
But I didn't like it.
So meanwhile, my brother-in-law-- shall rest in peace-- could not agree with his partners.
He was a butcher.
And we went in wholesale ourself.
It wasn't butcher for retail.
Wholesale, delivering through the manufacturers, some sausage and this.
And we went together.
And I was with him till 1960, another year.
And I start buying parcels of land to build houses.
When I had the first several parcels, I decided I have enough butcher business.
And I started building homes in New York.
That's how I became a builder.
Actually, I was not a builder.
I did not know too much about building, I have to admit.
I hired a general contractor.
And we agreed $16,500 for the construction.
I remember like today.
When we came to the contract, he said he changed his mind.
He needs $1,500 more.
I-- and his name was Bernstein.
I said, Mr.
Bernstein, you know what?
As long you increasing now, at the contract, you relieve me from mine obligation.
I changed my  mind.
I'll do it myself.
So I went out to construction fields.
And people were building.
I took business card from the carpenter, from the plumber, from the electrician.
I assembled everything and started building myself.
The first house, the cost was a little over $14,000.
That one was two-family houses.
And that's how I start building homes.
And I was very successful.
Wintertime, when it was cold in New York, I went with my wife, and my brother-in-law and sister, we went to Puerto Rico for vacation.
I say it's a lovely climate.
It's nice and warm.
We went for a ride to see construction, goes out.
We connected with a Jewish fellow from Cuba named Morris Demel.
Demel, D-E-M-E-L.
And we start-- we created a company with the name April Industries, like the month of April.
And we start building in Puerto Rico.
This was from 1970, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Puerto Rico, already building with the experience I got, already, from New York.
We built an average of 1,000 to 1,500 homes a year, mass production.
We hired one of the eight auditors with the name [INAUDIBLE] in New York.
Eight-- if you want to go public, you have to have one of the top eight.
Senator Javits was our law firm in New York.
That's where he helped me to get my brother in.
And after three years, our average profit was a million and over, net, every year for the corporation.
We went public.
And we sold, successful, the stocks.
So at the meeting before this stock was finally-- before the bell when you sell the stock, we had a meeting with the underwriters from Wall Street.
One of the underwriters ask me, Mr.
Aptekar, would you mind telling us which financial school you finished?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I said, why not?
The name is Lysa Gora.
It was-- everybody was quiet.
Nobody understood what I answered.
I said, I was hiding in the-- in the woods with a mountain, the name, Lysa Gora.
Lysa is like a bald head with no trees on it.
And that's the school, what I finished.
And the stocks were successful sold.
[LAUGHS]
Yes.
The oldest is 21, in Austin, college.
Second one is 20, which is North Carolina.
Goes for doctor.
They're both going for doctors.
Then I have-- I go on the line.
I have from my younger daughter, I have two girls.
Danieli, 16.
And Michell is 14.
And I have another grandson by the name Jacob.
He is 14.
And from my son, two.
Five-year-old, Clayton, and two years, Gavriel.
My mother is in Florida in the old-age home.
And she still-- she is now 97.
She still talk to me on the telephone.
And she still calls me by the name [INAUDIBLE], like I was very small.
She always asks Gavriel, then, when you come over?
Even I was there last week.
She already, this week, ask me, when you come over again?
[LAUGHS]
September 16 she passed away.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]
She was sick, two-and-a-half years, ovarian cancer.
Yes.
Not-- I wouldn't-- well, it's difficult to say yes and difficult to say no.
I support synagogues.
I sent my kids to religious school.
My boy was going to the Yeshiva [INAUDIBLE], in New  York, later Brandeis, a Jewish school.
They have a kosher home.
And they're proud of it.
I go to the synagogue once in a while.
But when I remember what happened, I somehow cannot agree with it.
And I don't know if it's right or wrong.
So I am between yes and no.
Never bend down.
Keep your head up.
And never be afraid to fight.
And don't let yourself be pulled to any ghettos.
Don't let yourself to be degraded.
You capable.
Israel show that we capable.
And I know we are capable.
If we have to, we are like anybody else.
Maybe we're not smarter from somebody else.
But we're not stupid.
More or less, we've-- we've been through everything.
It's difficult to remember the daily appearances, the daily encounters, what I had by escaping, chasing, jumping through fences.
It was impossible to go through.
Some-- The fact that I don't know where the energy I had, but being undernourished and be able to run fast that nobody can caught on with me.
It's-- it's a power which is-- you cannot even imagine.
Or to be able to climb in trees.
I was never a climber.
It wasn't my profession.
But you know it.
You have to know it.
And you deal with it.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I was always a survivor.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And even now, I love to work a lot.
And I always provided for the family.
I didn't let my wife to work.
Until Houston, she never worked here.
Here she said, I'm not sitting home.
The kids are gone.
I have to work.
[LAUGHS]
So I opened a store especially for her, for perfumes, two blocks away from here.
It still operates, but without-- unfortunately, without her.
Thank you so much.
You are welcome.
My pleasure.
Thank you.
